mission.title: metallb: Failover time very high in layer2 mode
mission.description: Failover time very high in layer2 mode. Community-reported issue.

steps.title: Identify metallb troubleshoot symptoms
steps.description:
Check for the issue in your metallb deployment:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n metallb-system -l app.kubernetes.io/name=metallb
kubectl logs -l app.kubernetes.io/name=metallb -n metallb-system --tail=100 | grep -i error
```
Look for errors or warnings in the logs that may indicate the issue.

steps.title: Review metallb configuration
steps.description:
Inspect the MetalLB configuration and check for failover behavior:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n metallb-system -o wide
kubectl get ipaddresspools.metallb.io -n metallb-system
kubectl logs -l component=speaker -n metallb-system --tail=50
```
In Layer 2 mode, failover time depends on the Kubernetes node monitor grace period (default: 40s). When a node hosting the leader speaker goes down, MetalLB must wait for Kubernetes to mark the node as unhealthy before electing a new leader.

steps.title: Apply the fix for Failover time very high in layer2 mode
steps.description:
The failover time in Layer 2 mode is primarily governed by the Kubernetes `--node-monitor-grace-period` setting (default: 40s). To reduce failover time:

1. Adjust the kube-controller-manager setting:
```
--node-monitor-grace-period duration     Default: 40s
```
This controls how long Kubernetes waits before marking an unresponsive node as unhealthy.

2. The total failover time includes:
   - Node monitor grace period (~40s default)
   - MetalLB leader re-election time
   - ARP/NDP announcement propagation

To test failover behavior, you can simulate a node failure and measure recovery time.

steps.title: Confirm Failover time very high in layer2 mode is resolved
steps.description:
Verify the fix by checking that the original error no longer occurs:
```bash
kubectl logs -l app.kubernetes.io/name=metallb -n metallb-system --tail=50 --since=5m
kubectl get events -n metallb-system --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' | tail -10
```
Confirm that the issue symptoms are gone.

resolution.summary:
Yes, it should per default take around 40 secs to detect unhealthy nodes. The relevant setting is https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/command-line-tools-reference/kube-controller-manager/#options 

```
--node-monitor-grace-period duration     Default: 40s
--
  | Amount of time which we allow running Node to be unresponsive before marking it unhealthy.
codeSnippets:
--node-monitor-grace-period duration     Default: 40s
--
  | Amount of time which we allow running Node to be unresponsive before marking it unhealthy. Must be N times more than kubelet's nodeStatusUpdateFrequency, where N means number of retries allowed for kubelet to post node status.

prerequisites.description: A running Kubernetes cluster with metallb installed or the issue environment reproducible.
